_meta.instrument: Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
_meta.note: This file encodes the analytical structure of the CRPD as machine-readable objects. It represents the normative standard against which domestic law is assessed. Only key analytical articles are included in v2.0; full article set is planned for v3.0.

general_principles.title: General principles

principles.label: dignity
principles.text: Respect for inherent dignity, individual autonomy including the freedom to make one's own choices, and independence of persons

principles.label: participation
principles.text: Full and effective participation and inclusion in society

principles.label: respect_for_difference
principles.text: Respect for difference and acceptance of persons with disabilities as part of human diversity and humanity

principles.label: evolving_capacities_children
principles.text: Respect for the evolving capacities of children with disabilities and respect for the right of children with disabilities to preserve their identities

red_flags.pattern: Rights language without autonomy, participation or accessibility logic
red_flags.rationale: General principles function as interpretive lenses for all other CRPD provisions; their absence from the domestic instrument's structure indicates non-integration of the treaty's conceptual framework.

general_principles.notes: Art. 3 principles are not stand-alone obligations; they are cross-cutting interpretive criteria applied throughout all other articles.

articles.title: General obligations

minimum_standards.description: States parties must adopt all appropriate legislative, administrative and other measures for the implementation of the rights recognised in the CRPD.

States parties must take all appropriate measures, including legislation, to modify or abolish existing laws, regulations, customs and practices that constitute discrimination against persons with disabilities.
Obligations extend to all parts of federal states without limitations or exceptions.

In regard to economic, social and cultural rights, States parties undertake to take measures to the maximum of available resources with a view to progressive realisation, without prejudice to immediately applicable obligations.
States parties must closely consult with and actively involve persons with disabilities, through their representative organisations, in decision-making processes.

red_flags.pattern: Declaration without reform pathway or timeline
red_flags.rationale: Art. 4(1)(a) requires concrete legislative and administrative measures, not aspirational language.

red_flags.pattern: No private-actor duties or extension to private entities
red_flags.rationale: Art. 4(1)(e) explicitly requires states to take all appropriate measures to eliminate discrimination by any person, organisation or private enterprise.

red_flags.pattern: Subject to budgetary availability without baseline guarantee
red_flags.rationale: Budgetary conditionality without a minimum baseline violates the immediately applicable core obligations within Art. 4(2).

red_flags.pattern: No consultation with disabled persons' organisations
red_flags.rationale: Art. 4(3) mandates close consultation and active involvement of persons with disabilities; absence of this mechanism is a structural gap.

articles.notes: Art. 4 general obligations are cross-cutting; their absence from domestic implementation architecture is a structural gap affecting all substantive articles.

minimum_standards.description: All persons are equal before and under the law and are entitled without any discrimination to equal protection and equal benefit of the law.
Denial of reasonable accommodation constitutes discrimination on the basis of disability.
Both direct and indirect discrimination are prohibited. Indirect discrimination arises when facially neutral measures produce a disproportionately adverse effect on persons with disabilities.

red_flags.pattern: Formal equality only; no indirect discrimination analysis or prohibition
red_flags.rationale: Formal equality is insufficient under CRPD. GC No. 6 (2018) establishes that substantive equality requires differentiated treatment that corrects pre-existing disadvantage.

red_flags.pattern: Reasonable accommodation not defined or not classified as non-discrimination duty
red_flags.rationale: Art. 5(3) is explicit: denial of reasonable accommodation IS discrimination. Framing accommodation as a discretionary benefit is incompatible with this standard.

red_flags.pattern: No multiple or intersectional discrimination recognition
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 3 (2016) and GC No. 6 (2018) both address multiple discrimination as a distinct form requiring specific measures.

minimum_standards.description: States parties recognise that women and girls with disabilities are subject to multiple discrimination and take measures to ensure their full and equal enjoyment of all human rights and fundamental freedoms.

States parties take all appropriate measures to ensure the full development, advancement and empowerment of women with disabilities, for the purpose of guaranteeing them the exercise and enjoyment of the human rights set out in the CRPD.

red_flags.pattern: Gender-neutral disability law with no disaggregated provisions for women
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 3 (2016) establishes that gender-neutral disability law is insufficient: multiple discrimination requires targeted measures, not just formal inclusion.

red_flags.pattern: No reference to gender-based violence against women with disabilities
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 3 identifies gender-based violence as a pervasive structural issue for women with disabilities requiring specific legislative recognition.

red_flags.pattern: Disability law and gender equality law developed in parallel silos with no integration mechanism
red_flags.rationale: Legislative parallelism without cross-referencing or coordination reproduces multiple discrimination at the institutional level.

minimum_standards.description: The best interests of the child shall be a primary consideration in all actions concerning children with disabilities.
Children with disabilities have the right to express their views freely on all matters affecting them, their views being given due weight in accordance with their age and maturity.

red_flags.pattern: No child-specific guarantees; disability law is adult-centred
red_flags.rationale: Children with disabilities face specific vulnerabilities (institutionalisation, special education segregation, denial of voice) requiring targeted provisions.

red_flags.pattern: Best-interests standard absent from disability-specific provisions
red_flags.rationale: Art. 7(2) makes best-interests a primary consideration in ALL actions concerning children with disabilities, not just child welfare proceedings.

minimum_standards.description: Accessibility is a precondition for the enjoyment of all CRPD rights, not an ancillary accommodation measure. It must be guaranteed ex ante (by design), not post hoc.
Accessibility obligations apply to both public and relevant private actors providing public facilities and services.
Accessibility covers: physical environment, transportation, information and communications (including ICT), and other facilities and services open to the public, in both urban and rural areas.
States must establish minimum accessibility standards with monitoring, enforcement and sanction mechanisms.

red_flags.pattern: Accessibility framed as optional, aspirational, or subject to progressive realisation only
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 2 (2014) establishes that accessibility is an immediately applicable obligation, not subject to progressive realisation, because it is a precondition for all other rights.

red_flags.pattern: Accessibility covers only physical environment; ICT and information excluded
red_flags.rationale: Art. 9(1)(b) explicitly covers information and communications, including ICT.

red_flags.pattern: No sanction or enforcement mechanism for accessibility violations
red_flags.rationale: Without enforcement, accessibility standards are declaratory. GC No. 2, para. 25, requires effective monitoring and remedies.

red_flags.pattern: Rural areas excluded from accessibility requirements
red_flags.rationale: Art. 9(1) explicitly covers both urban and rural areas. Rural exclusion disproportionately affects intersecting groups (rurality, indigenous communities).

articles.title: Equal recognition before the law

minimum_standards.description: Persons with disabilities have the right to recognition everywhere as persons before the law.
Persons with disabilities enjoy legal capacity on an equal basis with others in all aspects of life.
States parties must provide access to the support persons with disabilities may require in exercising their legal capacity.

Supported decision-making replaces substituted decision-making. Support arrangements must include: respect for rights/will/preferences of the person; absence of conflict of interest/undue influence; proportionality and tailoring; application for shortest time possible; regular review by independent authority.

red_flags.pattern: Guardianship, tutela, curatorship or other substituted decision-making regimes
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 1 (2014), para. 26: substituted decision-making regimes must be abolished and replaced with supported decision-making. Guardianship constitutes a denial of legal capacity in violation of Art. 12.

red_flags.pattern: Interdiction (incapacitación, Entmündigung) or partial incapacitation
red_flags.rationale: All forms of legal incapacitation deny equal recognition before the law, regardless of whether partial or full.

red_flags.pattern: Mental health or psychosocial disability used as criterion for restricting legal capacity
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 1, para. 13: legal capacity cannot be restricted on the basis of a mental or psychosocial disability, whether assessed functionally or status-based.

red_flags.pattern: No transition from substituted to supported decision-making with timeline
red_flags.rationale: Reform without timeline is declaratory. States must provide a concrete transition pathway (GC No. 1, para. 28).

articles.notes: Art. 12 is consistently the most litigated CRPD provision in both Mexico (SCJN) and Switzerland (Federal Supreme Court guardianship cases). GC No. 1 is the primary interpretive authority.

minimum_standards.description: Persons with disabilities must have effective access to justice on an equal basis with others, including through procedural and age-appropriate accommodations.
States must promote appropriate training for those working in the field of administration of justice, including police and prison staff.

red_flags.pattern: No procedural accommodation in judicial or administrative proceedings
red_flags.rationale: Art. 13(1) procedural accommodations are not the same as reasonable accommodation (Art. 5). They are specific to the justice context.

red_flags.pattern: Inaccessible court infrastructure (physical, communicative) with no remedy
red_flags.rationale: Physical and communicative accessibility in courts flows from Arts. 9 and 13 together.

red_flags.pattern: Legal capacity restrictions impede standing or procedural participation
red_flags.rationale: Guardianship and incapacitation regimes routinely restrict access to justice; Arts. 12 and 13 are interlinked.

articles.title: Liberty and security of person

minimum_standards.description: The existence of a disability shall in no case justify a deprivation of liberty.

Persons with disabilities who are deprived of their liberty are entitled to guarantees in accordance with international human rights law and shall be treated in compliance with the objectives and principles of the CRPD.

red_flags.pattern: Involuntary institutionalisation or involuntary psychiatric detention based on disability
red_flags.rationale: Art. 14(1)(b) is absolute: disability is never a justification for deprivation of liberty. This applies regardless of whether deprivation is labelled 'treatment' or 'protective'.

red_flags.pattern: Disability-based criteria in civil commitment or involuntary hospitalization legislation
red_flags.rationale: Civil commitment on the basis of 'danger to self or others' combined with mental health diagnosis is incompatible with Art. 14(1)(b).

articles.notes: Note on Switzerland: the Civil Code provisions on involuntary placement (Art. 426 ZGB) have been reviewed by the Swiss Federal Supreme Court. CRPD compatibility is a live issue in cantonal practice.

articles.title: Living independently and being included in the community

minimum_standards.description: Persons with disabilities have the opportunity to choose their place of residence and where and with whom they live on an equal basis with others, and are not obliged to live in a particular living arrangement.
States must ensure access to a range of in-home, residential and other community support services, including personal assistance necessary to support living and inclusion in the community.
Community services and facilities for the general population must be available on an equal basis to persons with disabilities and be responsive to their needs.

red_flags.pattern: Institutionalisation as default or primary support model
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 5 (2017) establishes that any form of institutionalisation is incompatible with Art. 19, regardless of size or quality of institution.

red_flags.pattern: Family-only care assumptions without state support structure
red_flags.rationale: Placing the support burden exclusively on families (typically women) without a community support infrastructure reproduces structural discrimination.

red_flags.pattern: No personal assistance or supported-living scheme
red_flags.rationale: Art. 19(b) requires personal assistance as a specific support modality; its absence is a structural gap in the community living framework.

articles.title: Education

minimum_standards.description: States parties recognise the right of persons with disabilities to education. With a view to realising this right without discrimination and on the basis of equal opportunity, they shall ensure an inclusive education system at all levels.

Persons with disabilities must not be excluded from the general education system on the basis of disability, and children with disabilities must not be excluded from free and compulsory primary education, or from secondary education, on the basis of disability.
Reasonable accommodation of the individual's requirements must be provided in the general education system.

red_flags.pattern: Segregated special education as default or primary model
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 4 (2016) establishes that segregated special education is incompatible with the right to inclusive education. Inclusion means placement in the general system with support, not placement in a separate system.

red_flags.pattern: Integration without systemic support (dumping)
red_flags.rationale: GC No. 4 distinguishes inclusion (systemic transformation with support) from integration (placement without change to the system). Integration without support is insufficient.

red_flags.pattern: No individualised support or reasonable accommodation in general school system
red_flags.rationale: Art. 24(2)(c) requires reasonable accommodation as a component of the right to inclusive education.

articles.notes: Note on Switzerland: cantonal competence for education (Art. 62 BV) creates federal-cantonal tension. Federal framework norms must be assessed for their capacity to produce inclusive education outcomes at cantonal level.

minimum_standards.description: The right to work on an equal basis with others includes the right to gain a living by work freely chosen or accepted in a labour market and work environment that is open, inclusive and accessible to persons with disabilities.

States must prohibit discrimination on the basis of disability with regard to all matters concerning all forms of employment, including conditions of recruitment, hiring and employment, continuance of employment, career advancement and safe and healthy working conditions.
Reasonable accommodation must be provided to persons with disabilities in the workplace.

red_flags.pattern: Sheltered employment as primary or default model without open-market integration
red_flags.rationale: Art. 27 requires open, inclusive labour market access. Sheltered employment can be a transitional support but cannot substitute for open-market inclusion.

red_flags.pattern: No employer duties regarding reasonable accommodation
red_flags.rationale: Art. 27(1)(i) requires reasonable accommodation in the workplace as a non-discrimination obligation.

articles.title: Adequate standard of living and social protection

minimum_standards.description: States parties recognise the right of persons with disabilities to an adequate standard of living for themselves and their families, including adequate food, clothing and housing, and to the continuous improvement of living conditions.
States parties recognise the right of persons with disabilities to social protection and to the enjoyment of that right without discrimination on the basis of disability.

red_flags.pattern: Purely assistentialist framing; discretionary benefits without rights basis
red_flags.rationale: Art. 28 frames social protection as a right, not a charitable benefit. Discretionary schemes without entitlement structure are incompatible.

red_flags.pattern: No disability-specific costs coverage (additional expenses of disability)
red_flags.rationale: Art. 28(2)(c) specifically requires access to public housing programmes that take into account the needs of persons with disabilities; the additional costs of disability must be factored into social protection design.

articles.title: Participation in political and public life

minimum_standards.description: Persons with disabilities have the right to vote and stand for election, including by ensuring that voting procedures, facilities and materials are appropriate, accessible and easy to understand and use.

red_flags.pattern: Capacity-based voting restrictions or exclusions
red_flags.rationale: Any restriction on voting rights based on legal capacity status or disability is incompatible with Art. 29 and Art. 12.

red_flags.pattern: Inaccessible voting materials or polling stations without remedy
red_flags.rationale: Art. 29(a) requires accessible voting procedures as a positive obligation.

articles.title: National implementation and monitoring

minimum_standards.description: States parties must designate one or more focal points within government for matters relating to the implementation of the CRPD.
States parties must designate or establish an independent monitoring mechanism, taking into account the Paris Principles on National Human Rights Institutions.
Civil society, in particular persons with disabilities and their representative organisations, must be involved and participate fully in the monitoring process.

red_flags.pattern: Focal point within a line ministry without cross-cutting coordination authority
red_flags.rationale: A sectoral focal point cannot coordinate cross-cutting CRPD obligations. The CRPD Committee expressed concern about CONADIS's placement within the health ministry (CRPD/C/MEX/CO/1, para. 57).

red_flags.pattern: No independent monitoring body or monitoring body lacking Paris Principles compliance
red_flags.rationale: Art. 33(2) requires independence; monitoring by the same body responsible for implementation violates this standard.

red_flags.pattern: No formal participation mechanism for disabled persons' organisations in monitoring
red_flags.rationale: Art. 33(3) is a mandatory participation requirement, not a best-practice recommendation.

articles.notes: Note on Mexico: CONADIS institutional design has been specifically flagged by the CRPD Committee (CRPD/C/MEX/CO/1, 2014). Note on Switzerland: the monitoring mandate is distributed across EKFF/COMAL and cantons, with coordination gaps.
